title: Limits of Static Pretraining
concept: If the pretraining data is completely different from the target domain (e.g. web text vs medical logs), transfer learning performance degrades, requiring domain adaptation. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Domain Adaptation.'

exercises.prompt: What happens to a model's performance if its pretraining data is completely different from the target task?

options.text: It makes no difference at all.
The model will instantly learn the new task perfectly.

exercises.explain: Transfer learning works best when the pretraining data is similar to the target task. If they are completely different, the model struggles.

cards.title: When AI Gets Lost in Translation
cards.body: Pretrained AI models seem to know everything. But what happens when you drop them into a world they've never seen before—like reading complex medical logs or machine code? They get completely lost! Let's find out why static pretraining has its limits.

cards.title: What is a Domain Mismatch?
cards.body: Static pretraining is great, but it has limits. If your model is pretrained on standard internet data (like forums and news) but your target task uses highly specialized text (like raw medical health logs or complex legal codes), the model's performance drops heavily. This mismatch is called a domain gap.

cards.title: The Dessert Chef in a Sushi Bar
cards.body: Imagine training a master chef using only books about baking desserts. If you suddenly ask them to prepare a sushi feast, they will struggle. Even though they know how to work in a kitchen, the ingredients, tools, and rules are entirely different. This is what happens when a model trained on general web text is forced to read medical health logs.

cards.title: The Wikipedia Model in the ICU
cards.body: An AI pretrained on Wikipedia is asked to predict patient risks from ICU equipment logs. The logs are full of raw numbers, abbreviations like 'SpO2', and medical codes. Because the AI has never seen text like this, it makes constant mistakes, showing that static pretraining alone isn't enough.

cards.title: The 'Know-It-All' AI Myth
cards.body: Some people think that if an AI is pretrained on billions of internet words, it can understand any text. This is a myth! Specialized fields have their own vocabularies, abbreviations, and structures that generic internet data simply cannot prepare the model for.

cards.title: Mind the Gap
cards.body: When your pretraining data does not match your target domain, the model faces a domain gap that hurts its performance.

right.text: Raw medical logs, abbreviations, vital signs, clinical notes.

left.text: Casual web forums, Wikipedia pages, news articles.

exercises.prompt: What happens when you apply a model pretrained on general web text directly to raw medical sensor logs?

options.text: The model will instantly master the medical logs because they are both written in English.
The model will perform perfectly because web text includes medical dictionary definitions.
The model's performance will degrade due to the massive difference in vocabulary and style.
The model will automatically rewrite the medical logs to look like Wikipedia articles.

exercises.explain: Generic web data doesn't prepare a model for the specialized vocabulary, abbreviations, and formats found in medical logs.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A model pretrained on standard web text will always perform well on highly specialized medical logs without any extra training.
exercises.explain: Static pretraining has clear limits. If the new data is too different, you will need domain adaptation to get good results.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence to explain the impact of domain differences.
exercises.sentence: If the pretraining data is completely different from the target domain, the model's transfer learning performance [blank].
exercises.explain: When the training data and target task are too different, the model's transfer learning performance drops or degrades.

exercises.prompt: Which of these models shows the limitations of static pretraining on a new target domain?

options.text: Model A: Correctly parses all medical abbreviations and links them to patient diagnoses.
Model B: Translates medical acronyms into plain English automatically.
Model C: Confuses the abbreviation 'SpO2' with a social media typo and outputs nonsense.

exercises.explain: Model C shows the typical behavior of a model suffering from a domain mismatch, failing to understand specialized terms like 'SpO2'.

exercises.prompt: Why does a model pretrained on general web text struggle to analyze highly specialized medical logs without extra help?

options.text: The model automatically translates the medical logs into simple web text first.
The model's weights permanently lock up and refuse to make any predictions.
The model makes poor predictions because the words and patterns in medical logs are completely foreign to it.
The model instantly learns the medical terms because it already knows English.

exercises.explain: When a model's pretraining is too far removed from the target task, we need domain adaptation to teach it the new environment's language. Next up: Domain Adaptation.
